title: SigmaOpenPathsWalks
lean_code_with_comments:
set_option linter.unusedVariables false in
-- claim_3_23 -- start statement
theorem sigma_open_paths_walks
    (G : CDMG Node) (C : Set Node) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V)
    {w₁ w₂ : Node}
    (hw₁ : w₁ ∈ (↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V : Set Node))
    (hw₂ : w₂ ∈ (↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V : Set Node)) :
    List.TFAE
      [ ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsPath ∧ π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC,
        ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC,
        ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC ∧
          (∀ (k : ℕ) (vk : Node),
              π.vertices[k]? = some vk → π.IsCollider k → vk ∈ C) ]
-- claim_3_23 -- end statement
  := by
  -- Cyclic TFAE proof: (1 → 2), (3 → 2), (2 → 1), (2 → 3).
  tfae_have h12 : 1 → 2 := fun ⟨π, _, hσ⟩ => ⟨π, hσ⟩
  tfae_have h32 : 3 → 2 := fun ⟨π, hσ, _⟩ => ⟨π, hσ⟩
  tfae_have h21 : 2 → 1 := fun ⟨π, hσ⟩ =>
    Walk.sigma_open_to_path π hσ
  tfae_have h23 : 2 → 3 := fun ⟨π, hσ⟩ =>
    Walk.sigma_open_colliders_to_C π hσ
  tfae_finish
lean_code_without_comments:
set_option linter.unusedVariables false in

theorem sigma_open_paths_walks
    (G : CDMG Node) (C : Set Node) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V)
    {w₁ w₂ : Node}
    (hw₁ : w₁ ∈ (↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V : Set Node))
    (hw₂ : w₂ ∈ (↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V : Set Node)) :
    List.TFAE
      [ ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsPath ∧ π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC,
        ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC,
        ∃ (π : Walk G w₁ w₂), π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC ∧
          (∀ (k : ℕ) (vk : Node),
              π.vertices[k]? = some vk → π.IsCollider k → vk ∈ C) ]

  := by

  tfae_have h12 : 1 → 2 := fun ⟨π, _, hσ⟩ => ⟨π, hσ⟩
  tfae_have h32 : 3 → 2 := fun ⟨π, hσ, _⟩ => ⟨π, hσ⟩
  tfae_have h21 : 2 → 1 := fun ⟨π, hσ⟩ =>
    Walk.sigma_open_to_path π hσ
  tfae_have h23 : 2 → 3 := fun ⟨π, hσ⟩ =>
    Walk.sigma_open_colliders_to_C π hσ
  tfae_finish
lean_explanation:
The theorem `sigma_open_paths_walks` takes a `CDMG Node` value `G` (the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$, with `Node` the ambient vertex type carrying `[DecidableEq Node]`), a conditioning set `C : Set Node`, and the subset hypothesis `hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V` matching the LN's "Let $C \subseteq J \cup V$" verbatim. The `↑` is the `Finset Node → Set Node` coercion: `G.J` and `G.V` are stored as `Finset Node` for computability, and the coercion lifts them so the union and the `⊆` are computed at the `Set` level. The two endpoints `w₁ w₂ : Node` come with explicit in-graph hypotheses `hw₁ hw₂ : _ ∈ (↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V : Set Node)`, again preserving the LN's "Let $w_1, w_2 \in J \cup V$" reading.

The conclusion is `List.TFAE [P₁, P₂, P₃]` — Mathlib's "the following are equivalent" predicate over a length-3 list. This is the canonical Lean encoding of the LN's "the following three statements are equivalent"; every pairwise implication is recoverable via `List.TFAE.out i j`, and the proof itself uses the `tfae_have` / `tfae_finish` tactic pair to chain cyclic implications (`1 → 2`, `3 → 2`, `2 → 1`, `2 → 3`).

The three list items inline the LN's existentials as `∃ π : Walk G w₁ w₂, ...`. `Walk G w₁ w₂` is the `Type`-level walk-data structure introduced upstream (`def_3_4`, `Section3_1/Walks.lean`): a finite alternating sequence of vertices and walk-steps. Item (1)'s body `π.IsPath ∧ π.IsSigmaOpenGiven C hC` reads as the literal conjunction "$\pi$ is a path AND $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open". Item (2) drops the path conjunct. Item (3) adds the LN's strengthening "every collider's vertex is in $C$, not merely in $\mathrm{Anc}^G(C)$" as a second conjunct, inlined as `∀ (k : ℕ) (vk : Node), π.vertices[k]? = some vk → π.IsCollider k → vk ∈ C`. The `[k]?` is Lean's `Option`-returning indexed list lookup: `π.vertices[k]? = some vk` simultaneously asserts that position `k` is in range and names the vertex sitting there, making the universal range-safe without a separate `k ≤ π.length` side condition.

The `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` prefix silences the unused-binder warning for `hw₁ / hw₂ / hC` at the signature surface: the LN insists they be visible, even though the existentials' bodies hide their use inside the imported `IsSigmaOpenGiven` and the `Walk.nil w₁ hw₁` trivial-walk witness.
design_choices:
**`List.TFAE` over a length-3 list, not nested `Iff` and not three separate `_iff_` lemmas.** The LN says "the following three statements are equivalent" without picking a left- or right-associated pairing, and downstream chapters cite this proposition for *different* pairs of items — sometimes (1)↔(2) for path/walk interchange, sometimes (1)↔(3) for path-with-$C$-colliders, sometimes (3) alone. `List.TFAE [P, Q, R]` matches that symmetry exactly: `List.TFAE.out i j` lets each call site pick the pair it needs, and the proof can be written cyclically with `tfae_have 1 → 2 → 3 → 1` style. Three separate `_iff_` lemmas would pre-commit to one cyclic orientation that the LN never picks; a nested `P ↔ Q ∧ Q ↔ R` would force an arbitrary associativity choice at every call site.

**Existentials over `Walk G w₁ w₂`, not bundled tuples or sigma types.** Walks are `Type`-level data in this chapter, but the LN's "there exists … such that" is `Prop`-level. `∃ π : Walk G w₁ w₂, ...` keeps the existential propositional while reusing the existing walk data structure. When `w₁` or `w₂` lies outside $J \cup V$, the type `Walk G w₁ w₂` is empty and the existential is vacuously false — consistent with the LN's universal quantification over $w_1, w_2 \in J \cup V$.

**Inlined conjunctions, no bundled "C-$\sigma$-open path" or "all colliders in $C$" predicates.** A helper `def IsSigmaOpenPath := IsPath ∧ IsSigmaOpenGiven` would be a one-clause wrapper around an `∧`, failing the project's threshold for earning its own name; inlining keeps the LN's "is a path AND is $C$-$\sigma$-open" reading visible at the statement surface. Variant (3)'s strengthening is similarly inlined as an extra conjunct rather than promoted to a separate `Walk.AllCollidersIn` predicate — if a downstream row ever needs it as an independent hypothesis, extracting a helper is a trivial refactor.

**`hC` threaded into the signature, shared across all three TFAE items.** The upstream `IsSigmaOpenGiven` predicate (`def_3_17`) is parameterised over its own `(hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V)`, so threading the *same* `hC` through the outer theorem signature is the only way to make all three TFAE items syntactically about a single `C`. Otherwise each existential body would need its own subset proof and the three items would no longer visibly share their conditioning set.

**`hw₁`, `hw₂` as explicit hypotheses on the signature, not absorbed into a subtype of `w₁ / w₂`.** Two reasons. First, the LN's literal "for $w_1, w_2 \in J \cup V$" is preserved verbatim. Second, the corner case $w_1 = w_2$ is witnessed uniformly across all three TFAE items by the length-$0$ trivial walk `Walk.nil w₁ hw₁`, which needs `hw₁` in scope to construct; this lets the proof dispose of the $w_1 = w_2$ case in one line without splitting on endpoint equality. The `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` prefix silences the warning that `hw₁ / hw₂ / hC` look body-inert at the signature surface — a chapter-wide convention for theorems whose hypotheses are LN-faithful but only consumed inside imported predicates and witness terms.
